questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Work done by a constant force
questions.structural_anchor: Work done by a constant force
questions.question: What is the SI unit of work?
questions.explanation: The joule (J) is the SI unit of work, defined as \(1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ N} \cdot \text{ m}\). Watt is power, newton is force, kilogram is mass.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Work done by a constant force
source_trace.content_anchor: The SI unit of work is joule (J)

explanations_tiered.definition: The joule is the SI derived unit of work, equal to one newton-meter.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: One joule is the work done when a force of one newton displaces an object by one meter in the direction of the force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A joule is how much energy you use to push with 1 newton of force for 1 meter.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Joule is the SI unit for energy and work, derived from base units: \(\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-2}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of work: force × displacement.
misconceptions: Confusing joule (work) with watt (power) is common.
Thinking newton is the unit of work is incorrect.

glossary_entry.definition: SI unit of work and energy, equal to one newton-meter.

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of work?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the unit of work?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: SI unit of work, equal to N·m.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate joule to everyday examples: lifting an apple 1 meter requires ~1 J.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define work in terms of force and displacement.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The joule is the SI unit of work.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Work done by a constant force
questions.structural_anchor: Work done by a constant force
questions.question: A horizontal force of \(25 \text{ N}\) pushes a \(10 \text{ kg}\) box \(4.0 \text{ m}\) across a frictionless floor. How much work is done by the force?
questions.hint: Work = force × displacement when they are parallel.
questions.explanation: Work \(W = Fd \cos \theta\). Here \(\theta = 0^\circ\), \(\cos 0^\circ = 1\), so \(W = (25 \text{ N})(4.0 \text{ m}) = 100 \text{ J}\). Option D multiplies by mass unnecessarily; B uses wrong numbers.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Work done by a constant force

explanations_tiered.definition: Work done by a constant force is the product of force magnitude, displacement magnitude, and the cosine of the angle between them.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When force and displacement are in the same direction, work is simply force times distance.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You push with 25 N for 4 meters, so you do 100 joules of work.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since force is horizontal and displacement is horizontal, \(\theta = 0\). \(W = Fd = 25 \times 4 = 100 \text{ J}\).
reasoning_steps: Check if force and displacement are parallel.
misconceptions: Using mass (10 kg) to calculate work (250 J) is incorrect; work depends on force, not mass directly.
Forgetting that work requires displacement in the direction of force.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: \(W = Fd\) when force parallel to displacement.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy transferred by a force acting through a displacement.

flashcard.front: 25 N force pushes 4 m. Work done?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 25 N force, 4 m displacement, parallel. Find work.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that work requires displacement in the force's direction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate work done by a constant force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Work Done by the Gravitational Force
questions.structural_anchor: Work Done by the Gravitational Force
questions.question: A \(2.0 \text{ kg}\) ball is lifted vertically upward \(1.5 \text{ m}\) at constant speed. What is the work done by gravity? (\(g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2\))
questions.hint: Gravity acts downward; displacement is upward.
questions.explanation: Work by gravity: \(W_g = mgd \cos \theta\), with \(\theta = 180^\circ\) (force down, displacement up), \(\cos 180^\circ = -1\). \(W_g = (2.0)(9.8)(1.5)(-1) = -29.4 \text{ J}\). Gravity does negative work because it opposes the motion.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Work Done by the Gravitational Force
source_trace.content_anchor: Wg = mgd cosθ, negative when rising

explanations_tiered.definition: Work done by gravity depends on the vertical displacement and the angle between gravity and displacement.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When an object rises, gravity does negative work, removing kinetic energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Gravity pulls down, but the ball goes up. Gravity is fighting the motion, so it does negative work.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Force of gravity is downward, displacement is upward, so \(\theta = 180^\circ\). \(W_g = -mgd = -29.4 \text{ J}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Work by gravity is path-independent: \(W_g = -\Delta PE_g = -mgh\), where \(h\) is vertical height gained.
reasoning_steps: Angle is 180°, so work is negative.
misconceptions: Forgetting the sign of work leads to +29.4 J (option A).
Thinking no work is done because lifting is "against gravity" but work is still done by gravity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Rising: gravity does negative work. Falling: positive work.

glossary_entry.definition: Work done by gravity: \(W_g = -mg\Delta h\) for rising.

flashcard.front: 2 kg ball lifted 1.5 m. Work by gravity?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Sign of work by gravity when lifting?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast lifting (negative work by gravity) with dropping (positive work).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate work done by gravitational force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Work done by a constant force
questions.structural_anchor: Work done by a constant force
questions.question: A force of \(50 \text{ N}\) is applied at an angle of \(60^\circ\) above the horizontal. The object moves \(3.0 \text{ m}\) horizontally. How much work is done by the force?
questions.explanation: Only the horizontal component of force does work because displacement is horizontal. \(W = Fd \cos \theta = (50)(3.0)(\cos 60^\circ) = 150 \times 0.5 = 75 \text{ J}\). Option D uses \(\cos 0\); A ignores force component.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Work done by a constant force

explanations_tiered.definition: Work is the dot product of force and displacement: \(W = Fd \cos \theta\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Only the component of force parallel to displacement contributes to work.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Only the part of the force that points sideways actually moves the box and does work.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Horizontal component is \(F_x = F \cos 60^\circ = 25 \text{ N}\). Work = (25 N)(3.0 m) = 75 J.
explanations_tiered.advanced: \(W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = (50 \cos 60^\circ)(3.0) = 25 \times 3.0 = 75 \text{ J}\). The vertical component does no work.
reasoning_steps: Identify force magnitude, displacement magnitude, and angle.
misconceptions: Using the full force (50 N) without the cosine gives 150 J (option D).
Thinking only the vertical component does work is wrong; vertical does zero work horizontally.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Only force component parallel to displacement does work: \(W = Fd \cos \theta\).

glossary_entry.definition: Work = force × displacement × cosine of angle between them.

flashcard.front: 50 N at 60°, horizontal displacement 3 m. Find work.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 4 — Work at an Angle
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Force 50 N at 60°, displacement 3 m horizontally. Work?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that cos0=1 (maximum work), cos90=0 (zero work).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate work for forces at an angle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Work done by a constant force
questions.structural_anchor: Work done by a constant force
questions.question: Which formula correctly gives the work \(W\) done by a constant force \(\vec{F}\) causing a displacement \(\vec{d}\)?
questions.explanation: Work is the dot product (scalar product) of force and displacement: \(W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = Fd \cos \theta\). Option A is only valid when parallel; B is cross product (vector); D is dimensionally incorrect.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Work done by a constant force

explanations_tiered.definition: Work is the dot product of the force vector and the displacement vector.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The dot product yields a scalar: \(W = Fd \cos \theta\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Work is force times distance, but only counting the part of force that points along the movement.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The correct vector definition is \(W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}\), which accounts for angle automatically.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In Cartesian components: \(W = F_x d_x + F_y d_y + F_z d_z\).
reasoning_steps: Recall work is a scalar quantity.
It comes from the scalar (dot) product of F and d.
Cross product gives torque, not work.
misconceptions: Confusing dot product with cross product (option B).
Assuming \(W = Fd\) always (option A) ignores angle.

glossary_entry.definition: Scalar product of force and displacement.

flashcard.front: What is the vector formula for work?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the vector definition of work?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Dot product of force and displacement.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that dot product picks the parallel component.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define work in terms of force and displacement vectors.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Work is the dot product \(\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the kinetic energy of a \(4.0 \text{ kg}\) object moving at \(3.0 \text{ m/s}\)?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy \(KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = 0.5 \times 4.0 \times (3.0)^2 = 2.0 \times 9.0 = 18 \text{ J}\). Option D forgets the 1/2 factor; B uses \(mv\) (momentum).

explanations_tiered.definition: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, equal to one-half mass times velocity squared.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: KE is a scalar quantity, always positive or zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A moving object has kinetic energy. Faster or heavier objects have more.
explanations_tiered.advanced: KE is derived from work-energy theorem: net work = ΔKE. Units: \(\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2 = \text{J}\).
misconceptions: Forgetting the 1/2 gives 36 J (option D).
Using \(mv\) gives momentum (12 kg·m/s), not energy.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that doubling velocity quadruples KE.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate kinetic energy of a body.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in which quantity?
questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem: \(W_{net} = \Delta KE = \frac{1}{2}mv_f^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2\). Option A relates to impulse; B relates to conservative work; D is not an energy quantity.

source_trace.content_anchor: Work done by net force = change in kinetic energy

explanations_tiered.definition: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This connects force (via work) directly to changes in speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you do work on something, you change how fast it's moving — that's changing its kinetic energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from \(F_{net} = ma\) and kinematics: \(W_{net} = F_{net}d = mad = \frac{1}{2}mv_f^2 - \frac{1}{2}mv_i^2\).
reasoning_steps: Use kinematics to relate a, v, d.
Result is change in kinetic energy.
misconceptions: Thinking work changes potential energy always is false; work changes KE unless gravity is the only force.
Confusing work-energy theorem with conservation of energy.

glossary_entry.definition: The net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use examples: pushing a box increases KE; braking decreases KE.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply work-energy theorem to solve problems.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Net work equals change in kinetic energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the SI unit of power?
questions.hint: Power is the rate of doing work.
questions.explanation: Power = work/time, so the SI unit is the watt (W), where \(1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}\). Joule is energy/work, newton is force, horsepower is a non-SI unit.

source_trace.content_anchor: The SI unit of power is watt (W)

explanations_tiered.definition: The watt is the SI unit of power, equal to one joule per second.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Power measures how quickly work is done or energy is transferred.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A watt tells you how fast energy is used. A 60-watt light bulb uses 60 joules every second.
explanations_tiered.advanced: 1 W = 1 kg·m²/s³. Horsepower (hp) = 746 W, a non-SI unit.
misconceptions: Confusing watt (power) with joule (energy).
Thinking horsepower is the SI unit.

glossary_entry.definition: SI unit of power, equal to one joule per second.

flashcard.front: What is the SI unit of power?

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The watt is the SI unit of power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A motor does \(500 \text{ J}\) of work in \(10 \text{ s}\). What is its average power output?
questions.explanation: Average power \(P_{avg} = W/t = 500 \text{ J} / 10 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ W}\). Option A divides time by work; C uses work only; D multiplies work and time.

explanations_tiered.definition: Average power is the total work divided by the time interval.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Power measures the rate of energy transfer.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If 500 J of work is done in 10 seconds, the machine works at 50 watts (50 joules per second).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous power may vary, but average power gives overall rate.
reasoning_steps: Divide work by time to get power.
misconceptions: Using \(P = t/W\) gives 0.02 W (not an option).
Confusing work (500 J) with power (50 W).

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare a slow vs fast worker doing same work.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A \(2.0 \text{ kg}\) ball is dropped from a height of \(5.0 \text{ m}\). Neglecting air resistance, what is its speed just before hitting the ground? (\(g = 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2\))
questions.hint: Use conservation of mechanical energy: \(PE_i = KE_f\).
questions.explanation: Initial PE = mgh, final KE = \(\frac{1}{2}mv^2\). Set equal: \(mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 5.0} = \sqrt{98} \approx 9.9 \text{ m/s}\). Option C uses \(\sqrt{4gh}\); D uses \(2gh\) without sqrt.

source_trace.content_anchor: KE_i + PE_i = KE_f + PE_f, v = sqrt(2gh)

explanations_tiered.definition: In the absence of non-conservative forces, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a falling object, gravitational PE converts to KE, so \(mgh = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The ball's energy changes from stored (height) to moving (speed). Energy conservation gives the final speed.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Initial energy: \(E_i = mgh\). Final energy: \(E_f = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\). Set equal: \(v = \sqrt{2gh} = 9.9 \text{ m/s}\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Final KE = same = 98 J = ½ × 2 × v².
misconceptions: Using v = √(gh) gives 7.0 m/s (option A).
Forgetting to take square root gives 98 m/s (option D).

glossary_entry.definition: Total KE + PE constant if no non-conservative forces.

flashcard.front: Speed of object dropped from 5 m?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Speed of 2 kg ball dropped from 5 m?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Total mechanical energy constant in free fall.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show that mass cancels out — all objects fall at same rate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply conservation of energy to solve problems.
